flashcards.front: What is VM Escape and why is it the most critical VM attack?
flashcards.back: VM Escape is when an attacker inside a Guest VM exploits a hypervisor vulnerability (e.g., in virtual hardware emulation) to break out and access the host or other VMs. It's the most critical because it breaks the fundamental isolation property — giving full control of the host and all VMs. Example: CVE-2015-3456 (VENOM).

flashcards.front: How does VM isolation work at the hardware level?
flashcards.back: Four isolation mechanisms: 1) CPU Isolation — Intel VT-x/AMD-V provide separate execution contexts. 2) Memory Isolation — hypervisor prevents cross-VM memory access. 3) I/O Isolation — Intel VT-d/SR-IOV for device isolation. 4) Network Isolation — virtual switches and VLANs separate VM traffic.

flashcards.front: What is VM Sprawl and why is it a security risk?
flashcards.back: VM Sprawl is the uncontrolled proliferation of VMs that are created but never decommissioned. Security risk: forgotten VMs remain unpatched, unmonitored, and may contain stale credentials or sensitive data. Mitigation: lifecycle management with automated expiration, regular audits, and tagging policies.

flashcards.front: What are the key VM image (golden image) security practices?
flashcards.back: 1) Use only trusted sources (official marketplaces). 2) Pre-harden and patch before publishing. 3) Scan regularly for vulnerabilities and malware. 4) Store as read-only (immutable). 5) NEVER embed secrets — inject at boot time via cloud-init, Key Vault, or Secrets Manager.

flashcards.front: What are the security risks of live migration and snapshots?
flashcards.back: Live migration: VM state (including memory with secrets) transferred over network — can be intercepted if unencrypted. Mitigation: encrypt migration traffic, use dedicated management network. Snapshots: contain full VM state including encryption keys and session tokens. Mitigation: encrypt snapshot storage, control access, delete old snapshots.

flashcards.front: Why is micro-segmentation essential for VM security?
flashcards.back: Traditional firewalls are blind to east-west traffic between VMs on the same host. Micro-segmentation uses SDN to create fine-grained security policies between VMs, limiting lateral movement after a breach. Examples: VMware NSX, Azure NSGs. Combined with IDS/IPS for east-west monitoring.
